items.title: Kubernetes v1.36 Sneak Peek
items.summary:
<p>Kubernetes v1.36 is coming at the end of April 2026. This release will include removals and deprecations, and it is packed with an impressive number of
enhancements. Here are some of the features we are most excited about in this cycle!</p>
<p>Please note that this information reflects t
items.reason: Kubernetes 1.36 is a major upcoming release with many deprecations and enhancements; the wiki currently has no coverage of this version.

items.title: Announcing Ingress2Gateway 1.0: Your Path to Gateway API
items.summary:
<p>With the Ingress-NGINX <a href="https://kubernetes.io/blog/2025/11/11/ingress-nginx-retirement/">retirement</a> scheduled for March 2026, the Kubernetes networking landscape is at a turning point.
For most organizations, the question isn't whether to migrate to <a href="https://gatewa
items.reason: Ingress2Gateway 1.0 introduces the Gateway API as a replacement for Ingress‑NGINX, a significant shift in Kubernetes networking that warrants its own article.

items.title: Running Agents on Kubernetes with Agent Sandbox
items.summary:
<p>The landscape of artificial intelligence is undergoing a massive architectural shift. In the early days of generative AI, interacting with a model was often treated as a transient, stateless function call: a request that spun up, executed for perhaps 50 milliseconds, and terminated.</p>

items.reason: Running AI agents on Kubernetes via an Agent Sandbox is a novel architectural pattern that is not yet documented in the wiki.

items.title: Defending Your Software Supply Chain: What Every Engineering Team Should Do Now
items.summary: The software supply chain is under sustained attack. Not from a single threat actor or a single incident, but from an ecosystem-wide campaign that has been escalating for months and shows no signs of slowing down. This week, axios, the HTTP client library downloaded 83 million times per week and pre
items.reason: Software supply‑chain security is a core concern for developers; Docker’s guidance adds valuable, platform‑specific recommendations.

items.title: Gemma 4 is Here: Now Available on Docker Hub
items.summary: Docker Hub is quickly becoming the home for AI models, serving millions of developers and bringing together a curated lineup that spans lightweight edge models to high-performance LLMs, all packaged as OCI artifacts. Today, we’re excited to welcome Gemma 4, the latest generation of lightweight, stat
items.reason: Gemma 4 on Docker Hub illustrates the emerging practice of publishing AI models as OCI artifacts, a topic absent from the current wiki.

items.title: Docker Offload now Generally Available: The Full Power of Docker, for Every Developer, Everywhere.
items.summary: Docker Desktop is one of the most widely used developer tools in the world, yet for millions of enterprise developers, running it simply hasn’t been an option. The environments they rely on, such as virtual desktop infrastructure (VDI) platforms and managed desktops, often lack the resources or capa
items.reason: Docker Offload GA expands Docker Desktop’s reach to VDI and managed‑desktop environments; this new capability needs documentation.

items.title: Run and Iterate on LLMs Faster with Docker Model Runner on DGX Station
items.summary: Back in October, we showed how Docker Model Runner on the NVIDIA DGX Spark makes it remarkably easy to run large AI models locally with the same familiar Docker experience developers already trust. That post struck a chord: hundreds of developers discovered that a compact desktop system paired with
items.reason: Docker Model Runner on DGX Station shows how to run large LLMs locally; the wiki lacks a guide for this workflow.

items.title: Docker Sandboxes: Run Agents in YOLO Mode, Safely
items.summary: Agents have crossed a threshold. Over a quarter of all production code is now AI-authored, and developers who use agents are merging roughly 60% more pull requests. But these gains only come when you let agents run autonomously. And to unlock that, you have to get out of the way. That means letting
items.reason: Docker Sandboxes for autonomous agents is a fresh security‑focused feature that should be covered.

items.title: Securing the open source supply chain across GitHub
items.summary:
Recent attacks on open source focus on exfiltrating secrets; here are the prevention steps you can take today, plus a look at the security capabilities GitHub is working on.
The post Securing the open source supply chain across GitHub appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: GitHub’s open‑source supply‑chain security recommendations are highly relevant to the wiki’s security section.

items.title: Run multiple agents at once with /fleet in Copilot CLI
items.summary:
/fleet lets Copilot CLI dispatch multiple agents in parallel. Learn how to write prompts that split work across files, declare dependencies, and avoid common pitfalls.
The post Run multiple agents at once with /fleet in Copilot CLI appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: /fleet in Copilot CLI introduces parallel agent execution, a new developer workflow that the wiki does not yet describe.

items.title: Agent-driven development in Copilot Applied Science
items.summary:
I used coding agents to build agents that automated part of my job. Here's what I learned about working better with coding agents.
The post Agent-driven development in Copilot Applied Science appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: Agent‑driven development in Copilot Applied Science provides practical insights into coding agents, a topic missing from the current content.

items.title: GitHub for Beginners: Getting started with GitHub security
items.summary:
Learn how to secure your projects and keep them safe with GitHub Advanced Security.
The post GitHub for Beginners: Getting started with GitHub security appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: A beginner‑focused guide to GitHub security fills a gap for newcomers; relevance is solid but slightly lower than more advanced topics.

items.title: What’s coming to our GitHub Actions 2026 security roadmap
items.summary:
A look at GitHub Actions’ 2026 roadmap, outlining how secure defaults, policy controls, and CI/CD observability harden the software supply chain end to end.
The post What’s coming to our GitHub Actions 2026 security roadmap appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: The 2026 GitHub Actions security roadmap outlines upcoming defaults and policy controls, essential for future‑proofing CI/CD security documentation.

items.title: Security Bug Bounty Program Paused Due to Loss of Funding
items.reason: Node.js pausing its bug‑bounty program impacts the security community; the wiki should note this status change.

items.reason: Node.js 25.9.0 release notes contain new features and fixes that merit a version‑specific entry.

items.title: Developing a minimally HashDoS resistant, yet quickly reversible integer hash for V8
items.reason: A technical deep‑dive into a hash algorithm for V8 is valuable for low‑level performance documentation.

items.reason: Node.js 25.8.2 release information should be captured for completeness of the version history.

items.title: Node.js 24.14.1 (LTS)
items.reason: Node.js 24.14.1 LTS is a long‑term support release; documenting its status is important for users relying on stable versions.

items.reason: Vite 8.0.3 release notes contain changelog details that are useful for front‑end tooling documentation.

items.title: vitejs/vite plugin-legacy@8.0.1
items.reason: The plugin‑legacy 8.0.1 release adds compatibility information that should be recorded, though its impact is narrower.

items.title: vitejs/vite v8.0.2
items.reason: Vite 8.0.2 is another incremental release; documenting it maintains a complete version history.
